source_paper_title: New Combinatorial Insights for Monotone Apportionment
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Cembrano_2025, title={New Combinatorial Insights for Monotone Apportionment}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0817}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0817}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Cembrano, Javier and Correa, José and Schmidt-Kraepelin, Ulrike and Tsigonias-Dimitriadis, Alexandros and Verdugo, Victor}, year={2025}, month=oct }

Section 6 (Discussion), page 23.

Whether a similar result holds without violating the house size is open.
title: Achieve quota, population monotonicity, and ex-ante proportionality without varying house size
background_latex:
An apportionment instance is a pair \((p,H)\) with \(p=(p_1,\dots,p_n)\in\mathbb{N}^n\) (state populations) and \(H\in\mathbb{N}\) (house size). Let \(P=\sum_{i=1}^n p_i\) and define the quota of state \(i\) as \(q_i=\frac{p_i}{P}H\).

An (integer) apportionment vector is \(x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbb{N}_0^n\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i=H\). A (possibly randomized) apportionment method outputs such a vector for each instance.

Quota compliance means that for every instance and every state \(i\), the realized allocation satisfies \(x_i\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\).

Population monotonicity is the standard strong monotonicity requirement: when populations change so that the ratio \(p_i/p_j\) weakly increases in favor of state \(i\) (relative to state \(j\)), then it must not happen that \(i\) loses a seat while \(j\) gains a seat (formally, the method must avoid such pairwise monotonicity violations across any two instances \((p,H)\) and \((p',H')\)).

A randomized method is ex-ante proportional if for every instance and every state \(i\), the expectation equals quota: \(\mathbb{E}[X_i]=q_i\), where \(X\) is the random apportionment vector.

The paper constructs randomized methods that satisfy quota compliance, population monotonicity, and ex-ante proportionality by allowing the realized total number of seats \(\sum_i X_i\) to deviate from \(H\) (variable house size), while still meeting \(H\) in expectation.
problem_definition_latex:
Design (or characterize the existence of) a randomized apportionment method that, for every instance \((p,H)\), outputs an integer apportionment vector \(X\in\mathbb{N}_0^n\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i=H\) almost surely, and simultaneously satisfies:

1) Quota compliance: for all \(i\in[n]\), \(X_i\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely.

2) Population monotonicity (in the strong pairwise-ratio sense described above).

3) Ex-ante proportionality: for all \(i\in[n]\), \(\mathbb{E}[X_i]=q_i\).

Equivalently, determine whether such a method exists without allowing ex-post deviation from the house size \(H\), and if it does, provide a construction/characterization.

open_problem_quote: Whether a similar result holds without violating the house size is open.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: If the statement is false, one might search computationally for small counterexample instances \((p,H)\) and population changes that force a violation among the three constraints; feasibility can be checked by enumerating all quota-feasible allocations and solving a linear feasibility problem over distributions. However, the universal quantification over all instances makes automated refutation nontrivial and may require guided search over instance families.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate methods can be represented as algorithms (e.g., randomized rounding rules over quota-feasible allocations) with tunable parameters, and fitness can be measured by empirical violations of population monotonicity and deviation from ex-ante proportionality across sampled instances while enforcing \(\sum_i X_i=H\) and quota. Yet proving correctness (or finding a definitive impossibility witness) goes beyond optimization, so evolutionary search would mainly help generate promising constructions or small hard instances.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Cembrano et al., 2024) isolates a central tension in apportionment: deterministic population-monotone rules are essentially divisor methods (Balinski--Young), but divisor methods can violate quota; randomization over stationary divisor methods still leaves worst-case expected quota deviation linear in \(H\) (Proposition 4). The paper then shows that if one relaxes the fixed-house constraint ex post (allowing \(\sum_i X_i\neq H\) but keeping \(\mathbb E[\sum_i X_i]=H\)), one can simultaneously achieve quota compliance, strong population monotonicity, and ex-ante proportionality via randomized fixed-divisor methods (Theorem 2), with deviation from \(H\) controlled by concentration bounds.

The open problem asks whether the same triple of properties can be achieved while keeping \(\sum_i X_i=H\) almost surely. The only forward-citing work provided here, "Online Proportional Apportionment", does not resolve the static question, but it establishes a sharp impossibility threshold in an online model: for \(n\ge 4\), no randomized online method can simultaneously keep the per-step house size fixed, satisfy an ex-post quota-type guarantee at all times, and be ex-ante proportional at each step. Although online constraints are stronger than the one-shot setting and the paper does not incorporate the strong population-monotonicity axiom, its flow-based characterizations and impossibility constructions suggest that enforcing exact house size together with both ex-post quota and exact ex-ante proportionality may face fundamental obstructions beyond the variable-house approach.

At present, based on the supplied citation analysis, there is no known construction or impossibility theorem that settles the fixed-house, quota-compliant, population-monotone, ex-ante proportional randomized method in the classic one-shot model. Promising directions include adapting the network-flow/polyhedral characterizations used for house-monotone quota methods (as in the source paper’s Section 5) to incorporate population monotonicity constraints, or extracting static impossibility instances by compressing the online impossibility gadgets into two-instance comparisons that witness population-monotonicity violations.

sources.title: Online Proportional Apportionment
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies an online (sequential) apportionment model. Its main randomized result (Theorem 2) states: an online apportionment method that (i) allocates exactly the prescribed number of seats each step, (ii) satisfies global quota ex post at every time (i.e., for all parties and all times, cumulative seats satisfy \(A_i^t\in\{\lfloor V_i^t\rfloor,\lceil V_i^t\rceil\}\)), and (iii) is ex-ante proportional at every step (\(\Pr[i\in X^t]=v_i^t\)) exists for all instances if and only if \(n\le 3\). For \(n\ge 4\), impossibility follows from their deterministic lower bound (Corollary 1) that global quota online is achievable for all instances iff \(n\le 3\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a randomized apportionment method in the classic one-shot setting with fixed house size \(H\) almost surely, satisfying (1) quota compliance, (2) strong population monotonicity, and (3) ex-ante proportionality \(\mathbb E[X_i]=q_i\). This paper does not address that exact static problem or the strong population-monotonicity axiom. Instead it treats a different, online/sequential model and proves a sharp feasibility threshold (\(n\le 3\)) for simultaneously achieving an ex-post quota-type guarantee (global quota over time) and ex-ante proportionality while keeping the per-step house size fixed. Methodologically, it provides a constructive network-flow characterization of all such online methods (Proposition 2). These flow/rounding ideas are related to the original paper’s use of flows/lotteries to reconcile quota and expectation constraints, but they neither construct nor rule out the desired static, population-monotone, fixed-house method in general. At best, it offers related tools and an impossibility phenomenon in a stricter online setting, suggesting potential barriers but not resolving the open problem.
